region_of_origin: United States / Western (served as fast-food or Western set meal in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried chicken food product class (FOODON_00002764); this record is a composite set meal including fried chicken, french fries, and mashed potato. No single FoodOn URI covers the full set meal; the fried chicken component URI is used as the primary anchor, representing a closest_parent match for the overall dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite set-meal code for fried chicken with fries and mashed potato. A0FJP (fried chicken) is used as the primary anchor, representing a closest_parent match for the overall dish. The fries and mashed potato components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Western set meal. SNOMED CT clinical food coding does not extend to composite fast-food set meals at this level of specificity.

serving.description: 1 set meal (2 pieces fried chicken + 1 medium packet fries + mashed potato)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving consists of 2 pieces of deep-fried battered chicken (approximately 200–220 g), 1 medium packet of french fries (approximately 115 g), and a portion of mashed potato (approximately 120–130 g), totalling approximately 500 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (2 pc) with fries and mashed potato — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100 g are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite set meal (cr_id F08020024). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from HPB with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried chicken (NDB 05062) and french fries (NDB 11352) weighted by approximate component proportions, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of french fries and mashed potato — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for french fries (GI ~63–75) and mashed potato (GI ~70–87); composite GI estimated at 65 weighted by carbohydrate contribution of each component.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated as follows: french fries GI ~65 (University of Sydney GI Database, baked/fried potato products); mashed potato GI ~80 (University of Sydney GI Database, instant and fresh mashed potato); fried chicken batter contributes refined carbohydrate with estimated GI ~70. Weighted composite GI estimated at 65 based on approximate carbohydrate proportions per 100 g of the full meal (fries ~8 g carb, mashed potato ~5 g carb, batter ~3 g carb). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving (80 g for 500 g serving) / 100 = 52.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 459 mg per 100 g, equating to approximately 2295 mg sodium per 500 g set meal serving — exceeding the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this meal; if consumed, request no added salt, avoid dipping sauces, and do not consume the full portion in one sitting.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL 52 per serving) driven by refined carbohydrates from french fries, mashed potato, and wheat flour batter, combined with high fat content that impairs post-meal insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this meal due to its high glycemic load and fat content; if consumed occasionally, limit to a half portion, substitute mashed potato with a non-starchy vegetable, and monitor post-meal blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying oil), high sodium (459 mg/100 g), and high total fat (10.56 g/100 g) collectively elevates LDL cholesterol, promotes arterial inflammation, and raises blood pressure.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal; the deep-fried preparation, high sodium, and likely trans fat content make it unsuitable for regular or even occasional consumption in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; estimated trans fat and saturated fat promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; potato (nightshade) in fries and mashed potato may trigger symptom flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried foods and nightshade-derived sides; substitute with grilled chicken and non-nightshade vegetables to reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (2 pieces) with French Fries and Mashed Potato is a Western-style set meal commonly served in fast-food restaurants, food courts, and Western casual dining establishments in Singapore. The meal consists of two pieces of deep-fried battered chicken (typically drumstick and thigh or breast pieces), one medium packet of deep-fried french fries, and a portion of creamy mashed potato made with butter and milk. The chicken is coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried in cooking oil until golden and crispy, yielding a crunchy exterior with juicy, tender meat inside. The french fries are crispy and lightly salted, while the mashed potato is smooth, creamy, and mildly buttery. Per 100 g, this composite meal provides 200 kcal, 10.32 g protein, 10.56 g fat, 15.96 g carbohydrate, 0.53 g sugar, 1.29 g dietary fibre, and 459 mg sodium. The meal is energy-dense and high in sodium, saturated fat, and refined carbohydrates, making it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The high glycemic load (estimated GL 52 per 500 g serving) and deep-fried preparation are the primary clinical concerns. The meal is tagged as halal and is a common working-lunch option in Singapore's Western food stall culture. In Malay it is known as ayam goreng dengan kentang goreng dan kentang lecek, and in Chinese as 炸鸡配薯条和土豆泥.

Fried chicken 2 pieces with fries nutrition facts
Ayam goreng dengan kentang goreng kalori

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020024) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and typical deep-frying oil contribution, respectively. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is rated high for the HPB-sourced macros and medium for derived values (saturated fat, trans fat, GI); the composite record confidence is high given the primary source quality.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried chicken (NDB 05062, ~3.2 g/100 g saturated fat), french fries (NDB 11352, ~1.6 g/100 g saturated fat), and mashed potato (NDB 11371, ~1.0 g/100 g saturated fat), weighted by approximate component mass proportions in the 500 g set meal. HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is an estimate based on typical deep-frying oil trans fat contribution; HPB does not report trans fat for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown data for this composite set meal are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry. Individual component data could be used to estimate these values in a future enrichment pass. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 65, medium category) derived from University of Sydney GI Database entries for french fries and mashed potato; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as GI 65 × 80 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving / 100 = 52.0. 5. Although this is a Western-style dish, it is widely consumed in Singapore across all ethnic communities and is sold at Western food stalls in hawker centres and food courts. Local-language names have been provided in Chinese (炸鸡（2块）配薯条和土豆泥), Malay (Ayam goreng (2 ketul) dengan kentang goreng dan kentang lecek), Tamil (வறுத்த கோழி (2 துண்டு) பொரித்த உருளைக்கிழங்கு மற்றும் மசித்த உருளைக்கிழங்குடன்), and Hindi (तला हुआ चिकन (2 टुकड़े) फ्रेंच फ्राइज़ और मैश्ड पोटैटो के साथ) to support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore context. 6. Halal status is carried forward from the input record (dietary_tags: halal); this should be verified against the specific outlet's halal certification.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is a precise canonical English name. Local-language names are now present in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi in local_names, satisfying G6 for Singapore-context dishes. Q1 passes.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (200.0), protein_g (10.32), fat_g (10.56), carbohydrate_g (15.96) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020024, confidence 0.95). nutrient_source is fully populated. Q2 passes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.29), sodium_mg (459.0), saturated_fat_g (2.8, estimated from USDA SR Legacy), sugar_g (0.53) are all present as numeric values. Q3 passes.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (65), gi_category (medium), glycemic_load_per_serving (52.0), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation of the estimation basis. Q4 passes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_00002764 (fried chicken food product) is used as the primary anchor with closest_parent match type. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (chicken meat, fried) is used as the closest defensible anchor with closest_parent match type. SNOMED CT is null with an honest gap note. No codes are invented. Q5 passes.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers what the food is, how it is prepared, taste and texture, cultural context (Singapore food court / Western stall), clinical relevance (hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, CVD), key nutrients, and multilingual context. Q6 passes.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium-driven), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, high GL and fat), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern, saturated fat + trans fat + sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is correctly set to Type 2 Diabetes. Q7 passes.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 16 entries covering English variants, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi forms. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check performed: every value in aliases_by_language.english, aliases_by_language.chinese, aliases_by_language.malay, aliases_by_language.tamil, aliases_by_language.hindi, and aliases_by_language.romanized is confirmed present in the flat aliases array. Q8 passes.

Iteration 3: Added all local-language name strings to aliases_by_language (chinese, malay, tamil, hindi) and to the flat aliases array to satisfy Q8 aliases and multilingual synonym coverage.
Iteration 3: Added romanized_local (Ayam goreng dengan kentang goreng dan kentang lecek) to local_names and aliases_by_language.romanized and flat aliases array.
Iteration 3: Updated notes to document the rationale for providing local-language names for this Western dish in the Singapore context.
Iteration 3: Performed self-check — every string in aliases_by_language confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases array.
